source_title: #23 Ray Dalio: Life Lessons from a Self-Made Billionaire

paragraphs.speaker: shane
paragraphs.name: Shane Parrish
paragraphs.text: Welcome to The Knowledge Project. I'm your host Shane Parrish. On this episode, I have Ray Dalio. Ray is the founder, chair, and co-chief investment officer of Bridgewater Associates. Bridgewater started out of a two-bedroom apartment in New York in 1975 and has grown into the largest hedge fund in the world. We talk about why Ray punched his boss in the face, his principles for living and work, the nuances of Bridgewater's culture, technology, why so few people deal with reality, mental models, how to build an idea meritocracy, and how to make better decisions.

paragraphs.speaker: ray
paragraphs.name: Ray Dalio
paragraphs.text: He and I were drunk on New Year's Eve, and we had this playful, fun, challenging relationships, and I decked him, and he then crashed the car when he went home. I figured that I was going to lose my job, and then he came in with a black eye, and he had no problem with me. It was a moment, and it was fun. I had a lousy New Year's Eve, but no hard feelings. I was at a certain age, and I had a certain kind of spirit. I was a little bit wild at the time.

paragraphs.speaker: ray
paragraphs.name: Ray Dalio
paragraphs.text: I wake up and immediately check what the markets and the news are doing. I was in a stage where I used to run the company and then deal with the markets simultaneously. Now I'm just following the markets and the economics. It's more of a thoughtful type of existence. I've got a group of brilliant people. We're examining something that is happening. Everything happens over and over again. Everything's another one of those, so by being able to look at history and make the connections and examine how the mechanics of the cause-effect relationships are, that's what we spend a lot of time doing, and then we convert those rules, those principles into algorithms.

paragraphs.speaker: ray
paragraphs.name: Ray Dalio
paragraphs.text: I meditate, so I started in 1969. The Beatles had meditated and went to India, and I read about it, and I became curious. I took my $50 fee and my flowers, and I learned how to meditate, and it was one of the most important, greatest things in my life, because it allowed me to have an equanimity. When you repeat your mantra over and over again with your breath, it takes your mind away from your thoughts, and you go into your subconscious. That's extremely powerful, because so much comes from our subconscious mind. That's where our creativity comes from, that's what intuitions are. It opens a passageway between one's subconscious and one's conscious. It allows you to completely eliminate stress, and it fosters creativity.

paragraphs.speaker: ray
paragraphs.name: Ray Dalio
paragraphs.text: I started Bridgewater in 1975. From 1975 until 1982 was seven years of building a business, making a lot of good and bad decisions. Then in 1982, I had calculated that a number of countries would not be able to pay back their debts to American banks, and the American banks had 250% of their capital out to them in loans, so they were going to go bankrupt. I thought we're going to have an economic collapse. Lo and behold, on August 1982, Mexico defaults on its debts. I thought we were going into a depression. It turned out, if you look at the exact bottom in the stock market, August 1982, when Mexico defaulted, that was the exact bottom in the stock market. That's because the Federal Reserve printed money. But that was totally wrong. So I lost clients, and it cost me money, and I had to let everybody who worked with me go. I was so broke that I had to borrow $4,000 from my dad.

paragraphs.speaker: ray
paragraphs.name: Ray Dalio
paragraphs.text: It was a terrible experience, but it turned out to be maybe the most valuable experience of my life, because it changed my approach to decision making. I went from thinking, I'm right, to asking myself, How do I know I'm right? In other words, how do I triangulate? It gave me the humility I needed to balance with my audacity. And so, then it gave me an open-mindedness. From that point forward, everything was better. That raised my probabilities of being right, and allowed me to manage risk. That is the most valuable thing I learned. I learned the value of thoughtful disagreement. I think the greatest tragedy of mankind is to hold opinions in their head that are wrong, that they're attached to, and that they don't stress test. I learned radical open-mindedness. I learned how to balance risks better without reducing my returns. And I learned to look at history of things that never happened in my lifetime.
